us-politics.headline: US-Iran War Escalates: Threats, Rescue, Economic Fallout
us-politics.body:
Trump issued a blunt Easter Sunday warning to Iran, threatening strikes on power plants and bridges if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened. The threat came as the US confirmed it had rescued a service member whose jet was shot down over Iran - a rare piece of good news amid a widening conflict. Supporters point to Trump's willingness to apply maximum pressure; critics question the risks of escalating against a nuclear-adjacent state.

The economic damage is already spreading. Jet fuel costs are spiking, airlines are cancelling flights, and a former World Bank chief warns Iran's economy is collapsing toward a barter system. One retired Army general predicts the conflict wraps up in under 60 days. That timeline is contested, and the human and financial costs are mounting fast.

sources.title: Trump drops Easter Sunday f-bomb in new threat to Iran
Trump vows US will strike Iran’s power plants, bridges if Strait of Hormuz is not reopened
US rescues missing service member from downed jet in Iran: Trump
Former World Bank chief says Iran economy collapsing into ‘barter system’ amid conflict
Retired Army general predicts Iran war wrapped up in ‘less than 60 days’
Jet fuel costs skyrocket amid Iran war, exacerbating crisis for airlines, travelers
Trump says U.S. military has rescued airman shot down over Iran

us-politics.headline: Trump Fires Bondi, Faces Legal Setbacks and Election Pushback
us-politics.body:
Trump dismissed Attorney General Pam Bondi, a loyalist he appointed after his 2024 win, in a move that caught Washington off guard. NPR reports the firing coincides with Trump walking back several campaign promises, raising questions about the administration's direction heading into its second year.

On other legal fronts, a federal judge blocked the administration's push to collect race-based admissions data from universities. A separate ad campaign, backed by Republicans, is pushing back against Trump's stated interest in federalising election administration. The legal and political resistance is coming from multiple directions - including from within his own party.

sources.title: Politics chat: Trump fires Attorney General Bondi, seeks to break campaign promises
Federal judge blocks Trump push to collect race-based admissions data
New ad campaign speaks out against Trump’s threat to ‘federalize’ elections

us-politics.headline: Pope Leo Calls for Peace as Global Alliances Fracture
us-politics.body:
Pope Leo used his first Easter address to deliver a direct call for peace, with the US-Iran war and other conflicts as the backdrop. The message landed as a pointed contrast to the tone coming from Washington.

Meanwhile, analysts are questioning the durability of Western alliances. Spain's decision to ban US military aircraft from its airspace has prompted debate about whether NATO can function as a unified bloc when member states diverge sharply on US foreign policy. Separately, Trump and Vice President Vance are backing Viktor Orban in Hungary's upcoming election, a move the Washington Post frames as part of a broader alignment between Washington and nationalist governments in Europe.

sources.title: On Easter, Pope Leo delivers commanding message of peace to a world at war
The ‘post-global’ NATO: Spain’s airspace ban signals the death of universal alliances
In Hungarian election, Trump and Putin are backing Viktor Orban

us-politics.headline: Harris Eyes 2028 as Democrats Search for Direction
us-politics.body:
Former Vice President Kamala Harris is travelling and speaking in ways that Fox News and other observers read as early groundwork for a 2028 presidential run. She has not declared, but her public schedule and rhetoric are drawing scrutiny from both parties.

Democrats are also pushing back on ICE enforcement tactics. Black Democratic lawmakers have drawn explicit parallels between agents wearing face masks during arrests and historical imagery of masked law enforcement, framing it as an intentional intimidation tactic. The administration has not responded directly to that characterisation.

sources.title: In fight against ICE facemasks, Black Democrats point to history
Kamala Harris' travels and comments clearly point to 2028

au-politics.headline: Australia scrambles to secure fuel supply amid shortage fears
au-politics.body:
Japan and other Asian nations have assured Australia that normal fuel supplies will continue, as Prime Minister Albanese works to head off a domestic shortage. The assurances follow a period of acute supply anxiety that prompted NSW authorities to fine nearly 100 service stations $1,100 each for displaying misleading prices at the pump.

Farmers are among those feeling the pressure hardest. Some say rising fuel costs have made it difficult to cover basic expenses, with one describing the situation as "struggling massively." The crisis has exposed how thinly stretched rural operations are when input costs spike.

The government's diplomatic push appears to have steadied nerves for now, but the episode has sharpened debate about Australia's long-term fuel security and its dependence on imported supply chains.

sources.title: 'Struggling massively': Farmer finds it hard to put food on the table
Nearly 100 NSW service stations fined $1,100 each over misleading petrol prices amid fuel shortage crackdown
Asian countries assure Australia ‘normal supply’ of fuel will continue as Albanese focuses on averting shortages
Japan assures Australia will get 'normal supply' of fuel

au-politics.headline: Police negligence claim filed after officer killed by Dezi Freeman
au-politics.body:
The sisters of a police officer killed by Dezi Freeman are preparing to sue NSW Police for negligence, according to their lawyer. The case centres on whether police command failed in its duty of care to the officer before the fatal incident.

The lawsuit adds legal pressure to an institution already facing scrutiny over how it handles risk to frontline officers. Police forces have consistently defended their operational decisions in similar cases, arguing commanders cannot foresee every threat.

The outcome could set a significant precedent for how courts assess institutional responsibility when officers are killed on duty.

sources.title: Sisters of officer killed by Dezi Freeman plan to sue police for negligence, lawyer says

au-politics.headline: US airman rescued from Iran as nuclear diplomacy stirs
au-politics.body:
A US Air Force F-15 airman has been rescued from Iran following a special forces operation, after apparently coming down inside Iranian territory. The rescue adds a sharp operational dimension to an already tense diplomatic moment.

Separately, Iran has signalled openness to renewed dialogue with the Trump administration, with the Spectator reporting Tehran has extended what amounts to a diplomatic olive branch. Whether Washington treats the gesture as credible or tactical remains unclear.

The two developments together illustrate the volatile mix of military activity and back-channel diplomacy currently defining US-Iran relations.

sources.title: Iran has offered Trump an olive branch
US F-15 airman rescued in Iran after special forces operation

au-politics.headline: Easter weekend: crashes, quakes and sport across Australia
au-politics.body:
A seven-year-old girl died after an ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east, one of several serious incidents across the Easter long weekend. A magnitude-5.5 earthquake rattled a remote APY Lands community on Sunday morning, causing alarm but no reported injuries. The North West Coastal Highway reopened after Cyclone Narelle damage, giving Easter travellers a welcome route back.

On the sporting front, Tim Tszyu claimed a boxing title in Wollongong with a dominant performance over Denis Nurja. The Sydney Kings won a dramatic NBL overtime game against the Adelaide 36ers, then promptly broke the trophy. The Western Bulldogs beat Essendon in AFL but lost two more players to injury in the process.

sources.title: 7yo girl dies after ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east
Tim Tszyu dominates Denis Nurja to claim title in Wollongong
Australians find joy amid global uncertainty in Easter celebrations
Bulldogs down plucky Bombers but lose two more players to injury
Raiders' season lurches towards disaster as Knights run riot
Kings break NBL trophy after dramatic overtime win as 36ers blow late lead
Travellers celebrate as major highway reopens after Cyclone Narelle
Magnitude-5.5 earthquake 'morning wake-up call' to remote community

world.headline: Iran War Enters Day 37 as Hormuz Crisis Deepens
world.body:
US and Israeli strikes on Iran entered their 37th day as Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz sent shockwaves through European energy markets. Trump offered no concrete plan to reopen the waterway, while Italian Prime Minister Meloni warned the Gulf is fundamental to European security. Iran continued striking Gulf infrastructure, hitting power and water facilities in Kuwait.

Oman stepped in as a back-channel, holding talks with Tehran on restoring transit through the strait. Iran separately signalled Iraqi ships could pass, a small but deliberate carve-out. Former Iranian Foreign Minister Zarif proposed a peace roadmap, though Gulf states complained of being excluded from negotiations. Thousands rallied in Iraq against the war. Polling shows the conflict remains deeply unpopular with the American public.

sources.title: Trump offers no concrete plan as Strait of Hormuz closure shocks Europe
Iran’s ex-FM Zarif proposes peace roadmap; Gulf points at erosion of trust
Italian PM says the Gulf is ‘fundamental’ for Europe’s security
Oman, Iran discuss smooth transit in Strait of Hormuz, Muscat says
Kuwait’s power, water plants damaged as Iran keeps attacking Gulf states
Thousands rally in Iraq against ‘senseless’ US-Israel war on Iran
Trump seeks to end the war without it becoming ‘an abject failure’
Iran war: What is happening on day 37 of US-Israeli attacks?
Trump’s unpopular war on Iran
Iran says Iraqi ships can pass Strait of Hormuz as transits tick up

world.headline: US Rescues Two Pilots Downed Over Iran
world.body:
The US military rescued a second downed F-15E pilot from inside Iranian territory, with Trump calling the operation miraculous. Both rescues came amid active combat conditions, raising sharp questions about how American aircraft are being lost and how rescue teams are operating deep inside Iran.

Details remain limited. The Pentagon has not explained the circumstances of either shootdown or the mechanics of the extractions. The incidents confirm US aircraft are flying strike missions over Iranian territory and facing effective air defences.

sources.title: US pilot from downed F-15E plane rescued in Iran: What we know
What we know so far about rescue of US airman in Iran
‘Miraculous’: US rescues second pilot downed in Iran, Trump confirms

world.headline: Houthis Strike Ben Gurion; West Bank Tensions Rise
world.body:
Yemen's Houthis claimed a cluster missile attack on Israel's Ben Gurion airport, the latest in a sustained campaign of long-range strikes against Israeli targets. The attack signals the conflict's regional spread shows no sign of narrowing.

Inside the occupied West Bank, footage emerged of an Israeli military dog attacking a Palestinian man inside a mosque. Palestinian Christians marked a subdued Holy Week under Israeli movement restrictions, with access to holy sites in Jerusalem tightly controlled.

sources.title: Footage shows Israeli military dog attacking Palestinian man inside mosque
Israel’s attack on UNRWA is central to its genocide of the Palestinians
Under Israeli restrictions, Palestinian Christians mark quiet Holy Week
Yemen’s Houthis claim cluster missile attack on Israel’s Ben Gurion airport

world.headline: Canada Heads to the Moon; Ukraine Hits Russian Oil
world.body:
Astronaut Jeremy Hansen became the first Canadian selected for a lunar mission, joining NASA's Artemis programme for a flight around the moon. The announcement marks a milestone for Canada's space programme.

Meanwhile, Ukraine launched drone strikes on Russian oil infrastructure, hitting a port in Primorsk and a refinery in Nizhny Novgorod. Fires and fuel leaks were reported at both sites. In Estonia, rumours of a separatist movement in the city of Narva circulated online, though analysts dismissed the claims as Russian disinformation.

sources.title: Jeremy Hansen is the first Canadian to head to the moon
Ukraine hits port in Russia’s Primorsk, oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod
'People's Republic' rumors: Does an Estonian city really want to join Russia?

ai.headline: Google's Gemini Bets Big on Maps Integration
ai.body:
Google's Gemini AI is now embedded directly in Maps, handling full-day itinerary planning with enough competence to impress skeptical testers. The Verge's hands-on found it genuinely useful, a rare outcome for AI features that typically overpromise.

The integration signals Google's broader push to make Gemini a practical layer across its core products rather than a standalone chatbot. Meanwhile, Microsoft is running a parallel experiment in branding chaos - one analyst counted multiple distinct products all named 'Copilot,' raising real questions about whether the strategy builds recognition or just confusion. Both companies are betting that embedding AI into familiar tools beats asking users to adopt new ones.

sources.title: I let Gemini in Google Maps plan my day and it went surprisingly well
How many products does Microsoft have named 'Copilot'?

ai.headline: AI Fakes, Copyright Trolls, and a Folk Musician's Nightmare
ai.body:
A folk musician found themselves targeted by both AI-generated imitations of their work and a separate copyright troll operation - two distinct threats that arrived nearly simultaneously. The Verge's account illustrates how smaller artists face compounding legal and reputational risks that major labels have resources to fight but independent musicians largely do not.

Grammarly is also navigating AI-era identity problems. The Verge's 'sloppelganger' story details how the writing tool's AI pivot has created friction with its core user base, who trusted the product for a specific, well-defined job. Anthropic added its own pricing wrinkle, announcing Claude Code subscribers will pay extra for OpenClaw support - a reminder that AI tooling costs are still being figured out in real time.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
Grammarly’s sloppelganger saga
Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage

ai.headline: Security Leaks Hit BrowserStack and US Border Agency
ai.body:
BrowserStack appears to have an internal data leak problem - a researcher traced unique tagged email addresses back to the company, suggesting someone with access to user records is selling or exposing them. BrowserStack has not publicly acknowledged the issue.

Separately, Customs and Border Protection facility codes appear to have leaked through online flashcard sets, likely created by employees studying for internal exams. Ars Technica's report highlights a recurring and underappreciated attack surface: sensitive information casually digitized for personal convenience and left publicly indexed. Neither incident involved a sophisticated breach. Both were avoidable.

sources.title: Someone at BrowserStack Is Leaking Users' Email Address
CBP facility codes sure seem to have leaked via online flashcards

ai.headline: Linux 7.0 Slashes PostgreSQL Performance, Fix Unclear
ai.body:
An AWS engineer has reported that Linux 7.0 cuts PostgreSQL performance roughly in half under certain workloads, a serious regression for one of the most widely deployed database stacks in production infrastructure. The fix is not straightforward, and the timeline for a resolution remains uncertain.

The issue lands alongside a separate but telling data point: Ubuntu's latest release now demands more RAM than Windows 11, inverting a long-standing assumption that Linux runs leaner. Together, the two stories complicate the narrative that open-source Linux is the obvious default for performance-sensitive or resource-constrained deployments.

sources.title: AWS engineer reports PostgreSQL perf halved by Linux 7.0, fix may not be easy
Ubuntu now requires more RAM than Windows 11

btc.headline: North Korea Drained $270M From Drift in Six-Month Operation
btc.body:
Drift Protocol has confirmed that a $270-280 million exploit was the result of a six-month North Korean intelligence operation, not an opportunistic hack. Attackers spent months embedding themselves before executing the theft, marking one of the most methodical state-sponsored crypto heists on record.

The scale and patience of the operation signals a maturation in how nation-state actors approach DeFi targets. North Korea has long used crypto theft to fund its weapons programs, but the Drift case suggests its operatives are now willing to play a long game - studying protocols deeply before striking.

sources.title: Drift says $270 million exploit was a six-month North Korean intelligence operation
Drift Protocol $280M exploit took 'months of deliberate preparation'

btc.headline: AI and Quantum Threats Are Closing In on Crypto Security
btc.body:
Ledger's CTO warned this week that AI is actively worsening crypto's security landscape, lowering the barrier for sophisticated phishing, social engineering, and exploit development. The threat is no longer theoretical.

At the same time, Bitcoin faces a longer-horizon risk. With $1.3 trillion in assets secured by cryptography that quantum computers could eventually break, developers are racing to build quantum-resistant alternatives before that window closes. Ant Group's blockchain arm added another dimension by launching a platform letting AI agents transact autonomously on crypto rails - raising fresh questions about what attack surfaces that creates when AI and crypto infrastructure become deeply intertwined.

sources.title: AI is making crypto's security problem even worse, Ledger CTO warns
Ant Group’s blockchain arm unveils platform for AI agents to transact on crypto rails
Bitcoin's $1.3 trillion security race: Key initiatives aimed at quantum-proofing the world's largest blockchain

btc.headline: Bitcoin Stuck at $67K as Bearish Sentiment Hits Months-Long High
btc.body:
Bitcoin is grinding sideways near $67,000 while bearish chatter on social media climbs to its highest level in five weeks, according to Santiment data. Sentiment has not been this negative since the early months of the Iran war, yet price has held relatively firm - a dynamic some contrarian traders watch closely.

Not everyone reads the stagnation as a floor. At least one prominent trader argues new lows are a matter of time if Bitcoin cannot break out of its current range. Robert Kiyosaki, meanwhile, used the moment to renew his case for Bitcoin and gold, drawing a parallel to the 1974 decoupling of the dollar from hard assets.

sources.title: Bitcoin holds steady as sentiment hits worst levels since Iran war began
New Bitcoin price lows 'matter of time' says trader with BTC stuck at $67K
Bitcoin bearish social chatter reaches 5-week high: Santiment
Robert Kiyosaki recommends Bitcoin, gold as 1974 shift comes full circle

btc.headline: Crypto's Token Supply Glut Is Becoming an Existential Problem
btc.body:
The crypto market is drowning in tokens. New analysis argues that supply creation has dramatically outpaced genuine value creation, diluting returns and eroding confidence across the broader market. Projects continue launching tokens with thin utility, flooding liquidity and leaving investors holding depreciating assets.

The problem is structural. Without mechanisms that tie token issuance tightly to real economic activity, the market risks a slow bleed rather than a sharp correction - making it harder to identify and price genuine innovation amid the noise.

sources.title: Crypto faces ‘existential’ token problem as supply outpaces value creation

food.name: Miso-Glazed Aubergine (Nasu Dengaku)
food.description: Halved aubergines broiled until silky inside with a caramelised sweet-savoury miso crust
food.tip: Score the flesh in a crosshatch before broiling — it helps the glaze sink in and speeds up cooking by about 4 minutes

exercise.name: Sunday Staircase Pyramid
exercise.equipment: A staircase of at least 10 steps
exercise.description: Climb the stairs using five different movement patterns — walk, side-step, skip a step, backwards, and sprint — cycling through them in ascending then descending rep counts to build cardio and coordination without leaving the house

learn.topic: Taphonomy — the science of how things become fossils
learn.description: Taphonomy studies the journey of an organism from death to discovery, covering decay, burial, mineralisation, and the extraordinary biases this introduces into the fossil record — most life that ever existed left no trace whatsoever.
learn.why: It reframes everything you think you know about prehistoric life; the gaps in the record aren't random noise but patterned absences you can actually reason about, which makes deep time feel both stranger and more knowable
